*Name:* Pitsligo Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 15th century
*Coords:* 57.6923, -2.1069
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pitsligo_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Pitsligo%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Pitsligo_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7912882.jpg/330px-Pitsligo_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_7912882.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Google places is wrong Pittulie and Pitsligo castles are nearby but two different castles. 

*Description:*
Pitsligo Castle is a ruined castle half a mile east of Rosehearty, Aberdeenshire, Scotland. Modified in the 1570s by the Forbes of Druminnor, it was described by W. Douglas Simpson as one of the nine castles of the Knuckle, referring to the rocky headland of North-East Aberdeenshire. It is listed by Historic Environment Scotland as a scheduled monument.

_Listing 1 of 10_

*Name:* Cairnbulg Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 57.6656, -1.9736
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cairnbulg_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Cairnbulg%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Cairnbulg_Castle.jpg/330px-Cairnbulg_Castle.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Needs more research 

*Description:*
Cairnbulg Castle is a z-plan castle situated in Cairnbulg, Aberdeenshire, Scotland. It was described by W. Douglas Simpson as one of the nine castles of the Knuckle, referring to the rocky headland of north-east Aberdeenshire. It stands by the River Philorth and was originally known as Philorth Castle. The 17th-century Philorth Castle, an L-plan house consisting of a sizeable crow-stepped block, was demolished after a fire in 1915.

_Listing 3 of 10_

listing.desc: Cairnbulg Castle is a z-plan castle situated in Cairnbulg, Aberdeenshire, Scotland. It was described by W. The 17th-century Philorth Castle, an L-plan house consisting of a sizeable crow-stepped block, was demolished after a fire in 1915.

*Coords:* 41.5565, -72.8354
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castle_Craig
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Craig%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Castle_Craig.jpg/330px-Castle_Craig.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Needs more research. wiki coordinates are in connecticut? 

*Description:*
Castle Craig, located on East Peak in the Hanging Hills, is constructed of trap rock and is 32 feet (10 m) in height and has a base 58 feet (18 m) in circumference. A metal interior stairway is used to ascend the observation deck on top. It stands 976 feet (297 m) above sea level and provides an expansive view of the greater Meriden, Connecticut area.

_Listing 1 of 2_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Craig y Nos Castle
*Country:* wales
*Region:* mid wales marches
*County:* Powys
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 19th century
*Coords:* 51.825, -3.68416667
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Craig-y-Nos_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Craig%20y%20Nos%20Castle/@51.8361,-3.6861,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/places/ANXAkqF41aMvBoFGOgpoxdeAbnxZIVMIPdE-1FYlkdCf2urBwrzVMia6p79g0hV2E30yP4agakn4b-rhz_NHL8MZOwZdWEMrkpvGdbI=s4800-w719
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* mark as hotel. 

*Description:*
Craig-y-nos Castle, is a Scots baronial-style country house near Glyntawe in Powys, Wales. Built on parkland beside the River Tawe in the upper Swansea Valley, it is located on the southeastern edge of the Black Mountain. The castle, formerly owned by opera singer Adelina Patti, is now a hotel. Its landscaped grounds are now a country park, managed by the Brecon Beacons National Park Authority. They are listed on the Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales. The castle is a Grade II* listed building and its theatre has a separate Grade I listing.

_Listing 2 of 2_

listing.name: Craig y Nos Castle
listing.desc: Craig-y-nos Castle, is a Scots baronial-style country house near Glyntawe in Powys, Wales. Built on parkland beside the River Tawe in the upper Swansea Valley, it is located on the southeastern edge of the Black Mountain. The castle, formerly owned by opera singer Adelina Patti, is now a hotel. Its landscaped grounds are now a country park, managed by the Brecon Beacons National Park Authority. They are listed on the Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales. The castle is a Grade II* listed building and its theatre has a separate Grade I listing.

imageAttribution.description: Craig-y-nos Castle — a 19th century Gothic revival style castle in Wales.

*Name:* Inverallochy Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* tower house
*Period:* 16th century
*Coords:* 57.65641, -1.93308
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverallochy_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Inverallochy%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Inverallochy_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_8145389.jpg/330px-Inverallochy_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_8145389.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Inverallochy Castle is a ruined courtyard castle, near the village of Inverallochy in the Buchan area of North-East Scotland, dating to 1504. It lies 0.5 miles (0.80 km) south of Cairnbulg Castle, near Fraserburgh, and formerly stood beside the now-drained Loch of Inverallochy. It was described by W. Douglas Simpson as one of the nine castles of the Knuckle, referring to the rocky headland of North-East Aberdeenshire.

_Listing 2 of 10_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Kinnaird Head Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* tower house
*Period:* 16th century
*Coords:* 57.69754, -2.00399
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinnaird_Head
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Kinnaird%20Head%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/places/ANXAkqHFAtgwBMbYP_9ijlyof9eLs0gAgG0Dce6lbFUJqjVBvzNC5kfX8JBwPvsSsu-8ldq6jSNeSTse4E8rFlrdtgHKGS0kFjIkB0o=s4800-w768
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Kinnaird Head is a headland projecting into the North Sea, within the town of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, on the east coast of Scotland. The 16th-century Kinnaird Castle was converted in 1787 for use as the Kinnaird Head Lighthouse, the first lighthouse in Scotland to be lit by the Commissioners of Northern Lights. Kinnaird Castle and the nearby Wine Tower were described by W. Douglas Simpson as two of the nine castles of the Knuckle, referring to the rocky headland of north-east Aberdeenshire. The lighthouse is a category A listed building. and the Wine Tower. is a scheduled monument. The buildings around the base of the lighthouse are the work of Robert Stevenson.

_Listing 3 of 10_

listing.name: Kinnaird Head Castle
listing.desc: Kinnaird Head is a headland projecting into the North Sea, within the town of Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire, on the east coast of Scotland. The 16th-century Kinnaird Castle was converted in 1787 for use as the Kinnaird Head Lighthouse, the first lighthouse in Scotland to be lit by the Commissioners of Northern Lights. Kinnaird Castle and the nearby Wine Tower were described by W. Douglas Simpson as two of the nine castles of the Knuckle, referring to the rocky headland of north-east Aberdeenshire. The lighthouse is a category A listed building. and the Wine Tower. is a scheduled monument. The buildings around the base of the lighthouse are the work of Robert Stevenson.

imageAttribution.artist: Stanley Bruce (User: Bard o' the Broch)
imageAttribution.description: Fraserburgh Lighthouse (Kinnaird Castle) and the Wine Tower

*Name:* Baikie Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Angus
*Type:* tower house
*Period:* Medieval
*Coords:* 56.6304, -3.1112
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baikie_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Baikie%20Castle%20Angus%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/baikie-castle-2.jpg
*Gallery:* 3 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* I do not think this place exists. 

*Description:*
Baikie Castle, was a castle on the shores of the former Loch of Baikie, Angus, Scotland. The castle was owned by the Fenton family from the 13th century until the 15th century, when it was passed to the Lyon of Glamis family. The castle was surrounded by a moat, with a drawbridge and stone causeway providing access to the castle. No remains were evident by the 19th century.

_Listing 2 of 10_

*Name:* Castle of Fiddes
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 16th century
*Coords:* 56.92248611, -2.290225
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castle_of_Fiddes
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Castle%20of%20Fiddes%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/castle-of-fiddes.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* Needs more info 

*Description:*


The Castle of Fiddes is a 16th-century tower house in Aberdeenshire, Scotland, located around 6 kilometres (3.7 mi) south-west of Stonehaven. It is dated 1592 on a window lintel, with a later date of 1673 on the east wall suggesting a renovation at this time. It was the property of the Arbuthnott family, who sold it in the later 17th century to the Thomson family of Arduthie. It was modernised around 1930 and remains occupied. It is a category A listed building.

_Listing 5 of 10_

It is a category A listed building.

*Name:* Muchalls Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Aberdeenshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 17th century
*Coords:* 57.0175, -2.1798
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muchalls_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Muchalls%20Castle%20Aberdeenshire%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Muchallscastle.jpg/330px-Muchallscastle.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Muchalls Castle stands overlooking the North Sea in the countryside of Kincardine and Mearns, Aberdeenshire, Scotland. The lower course is a well-preserved Romanesque, double-groined 13th-century tower house structure, built by the Frasers of Muchalls. Upon this structure, the 17th-century castle was begun by Alexander Burnett of Leys and completed by his son, Sir Thomas Burnett, 1st Baronet, in 1627. The Burnetts of Leys built the remaining four-storey present-day castle.

_Listing 6 of 10_

*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Darnaway%20Castle%20Moray%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Darnaway_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_13190.jpg/330px-Darnaway_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_13190.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Darnaway Castle, also known as Tarnaway Castle, is located in Darnaway Forest, 3 miles (5 km) southwest of Forres in Moray, Scotland. This was Comyn land, given to Thomas Randolph along with the Earldom of Moray by King Robert I. The castle has remained the seat of the Earls of Moray ever since. Rebuilt in 1810, it retains the old banqueting hall, capable of accommodating 1,000 people.

*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/kHZiA1nY6FG77jLa7

*Description:*
Leith Hall is a country house in Kennethmont, Aberdeenshire, Scotland. It was built in 1650, on the site of the medieval Peill Castle, and was the home of the Leith-Hay family for nearly three centuries. Since 1945 it has been run by the National Trust of Scotland (NTS). Leith Hall is set in a 286-acre (1.16 km2) estate, with gardens included.

_Listing 10 of 10_

*Image Source:* google

*Description:*
Haddo House is a Scottish stately home located near Tarves in Aberdeenshire, approximately 20 miles (32 km) north of Aberdeen. Completed in 1732 to designs by William Adam, it was the former seat of the Earls and Marquesses of Aberdeen and Temair and has been owned by the National Trust for Scotland since 1979.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Sueno's Stone
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Moray
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 9th-10th century
*Coords:* 57.6157, -3.5977
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sueno%27s_Stone
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Sueno's%20Stone%20Moray%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/19/Forres_sueno.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Sueno's Stone is a Picto-Scottish Class III standing stone on the north-easterly edge of Forres in Moray and is the largest surviving Pictish style cross-slab stone of its type in Scotland, standing 6.5 metres in height. It is situated on a raised bank on a now isolated section of the former road to Findhorn. The stone is named after Sweyn Forkbeard, but this association has been challenged and it has also been associated with the killing of King Dubh mac Ailpin in Forres in 966. The stone was erected c. 850–950 but by whom and for what, is unknown.

_Listing 4 of 10_

listing.name: Sueno's Stone
listing.desc: Sueno's Stone is a Picto-Scottish Class III standing stone on the north-easterly edge of Forres in Moray and is the largest surviving Pictish style cross-slab stone of its type in Scotland, standing 6.5 metres in height. It is situated on a raised bank on a now isolated section of the former road to Findhorn. The stone is named after Sweyn Forkbeard, but this association has been challenged and it has also been associated with the killing of King Dubh mac Ailpin in Forres in 966. The stone was erected c. 850–950 but by whom and for what, is unknown.

Durris Castle, also known as the House of Dores, was an early royal residence on the southern bank of the River Dee in Aberdeenshire, Scotland. The site consisted of a medieval motte and bailey castle and controlled the northern end of the Crynes Corse Mounth trackway.

*Name:* Aberlemno Sculptured Stones
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* northeast tayside
*County:* Angus
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 7th-9th century
*Coords:* 56.68916667, -2.78138889
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aberlemno_sculptured_stones
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Aberlemno%20Sculptured%20Stones%20Angus%20scotland
*Main Image:* none
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
The Aberlemno Sculptured Stones are a series of Pictish standing stones originating in and around the village of Aberlemno, Angus, Scotland. Three are located in the village and a fourth, found in 1962, is on display in The McManus in Dundee. They date from the Early Medieval period. A fifth stone standing in the village has signs of carving, but is of unknown authenticity as a Pictish artefact.

_Listing 6 of 10_
